nodes.title: Diversified Funding Sources
nodes.text: SFF, EAIF, AISTO grants plus community donors providing operational funding

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Charity Commission filing (1189768) shows consistent operational expenditure 2020-2024; (2) Manifund project page confirms grants from SFF (2023), AISTO and EAIF (2024); (3) EA Forum updates document runway stabilization through diversified funding. Historical delivery of 20,900+ person-days demonstrates execution capability.
connections.assumptions: Funding diversification reduces risk vs. single-source dependence. Evidence basis: 2023 funding constraints (EA Forum) resolved through multiple grantmakers. Testability: Track funding source diversity and operational continuity quarterly. Risk factors: Economic downturns affect multiple funders simultaneously, mission drift to please diverse stakeholders, grant administration overhead increases with diversification. Historical precedent: EA infrastructure organizations show improved sustainability with diversified funding (CEA, MIRI examples).

nodes.title: EA Community Applications
nodes.text: Early-career individuals applying for residency focused on EA-relevant projects

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Website describes established application process with counterfactual impact evaluation; (2) EA Forum visibility and media coverage (The Economist 2018, Times 2018) generates community awareness; (3) Continued operations since 2018 suggests consistent applicant flow. Quality note: Application volume data not publicly available for verification.
connections.assumptions: EA community awareness translates to qualified applications vs. general interest without commitment. Evidence basis: Selection criteria focus on EA alignment and project quality suggests filtering process. Testability: Track application volumes, acceptance rates, and applicant EA engagement scores. Risk factors: EA community growth stagnation limits pipeline, reputation issues reduce applications, competing opportunities (other residencies/fellowships). Historical precedent: EA infrastructure organizations maintain applicant pools through community engagement.

nodes.title: Rigorous Selection Process
nodes.text: Competitive evaluation prioritizing counterfactual impact and project quality

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Website describes selection criteria focusing on counterfactual impact and work quality; (2) Successful alumni outcomes (AI Standards Lab, Convergence Analysis) suggest effective filtering; (3) Community testimonials reference high-quality peer environment. Quality note: Selection methodology not publicly detailed, success metrics self-defined.
connections.assumptions: Selection process accurately identifies individuals likely to benefit from program vs. selection bias creating appearance of success. Evidence basis: Alumni outcomes suggest some predictive validity. Testability: Compare selected vs. rejected applicant outcomes after 2 years with matched controls. Risk factors: Selection bias toward certain backgrounds/presentation styles, evaluator expertise limitations, gaming of selection criteria by applicants. Historical precedent: Competitive fellowship programs show selection quality crucial to outcomes (Rhodes Scholars, Gates Cambridge).

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Selection process creates curated community of EA-aligned individuals; (2) Testimonials emphasize value of high-quality peer networks; (3) Average 12 meaningful connections per grantee (website metric) suggests network formation success. Quality note: Network quality subjective, self-reported metrics.
connections.assumptions: Curated selection creates more valuable networks than random EA community sampling. Evidence basis: Testimonials consistently highlight peer quality as program benefit. Testability: Compare network formation and quality in selected vs. non-selected EA community members. Risk factors: Selection homogeneity reduces network diversity, small cohort sizes limit network effects, geographic concentration in Blackpool reduces global network reach. Historical precedent: Elite educational programs show selection quality affects peer network value.

nodes.title: Subsidized 90-Day Residencies
nodes.text: Free/low-cost accommodation and meals for up to 90 days, extendable to 12 months

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) 80% of residents report increased productivity during stays (internal surveys); (2) Historical cost-effectiveness: £6,000/person-year (2019 estimates) vs. typical EA salary opportunity costs; (3) Testimonials reference focused work time without financial stress. Quality note: Productivity self-reported, limited counterfactual analysis of what residents would have accomplished otherwise.
connections.assumptions: Removing financial pressure enables skill development vs. alternative uses of time/money. Evidence basis: Testimonials consistently reference ability to focus without financial stress. Testability: Compare skill development and project completion rates between full subsidies vs. partial subsidies vs. external control groups. Risk factors: Dependency creation rather than empowerment, selection effects (motivated individuals succeed regardless), opportunity costs of extended residence vs. immediate employment. Historical precedent: Writer residencies and academic sabbaticals show mixed results - benefits highly dependent on individual motivation and project clarity.

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Case studies of specific projects completed during residencies (research papers, organizational launches); (2) 20,900+ person-days of work completed historically; (3) Examples of residents launching organizations (Electric Sheep, Orxl, ML4G). Quality note: Project quality varies significantly, completion rates not systematically tracked.
connections.assumptions: Extended time allocation leads to higher-quality project completion vs. normal time constraints actually improving focus and efficiency. Evidence basis: Case studies suggest some high-quality outputs, but base rates unclear. Testability: Compare project completion rates and quality assessments between 90-day vs. shorter residencies vs. external work. Risk factors: Extended timeframes enable procrastination, projects expand to fill available time, isolation from professional feedback reduces quality, lack of external deadlines reduces urgency. Historical precedent: Research sabbaticals show time extension benefits plateau and can become counterproductive without structure.

nodes.title: Collaborative Community Environment
nodes.text: Shared workspace, communal meals, and informal networking opportunities

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Average 12 meaningful connections per grantee (website metric); (2) The Economist (2018) highlights communal dining as central feature; (3) Testimonials consistently reference valuable peer interactions and collaborative opportunities. Quality note: Connection quality subjective, long-term network value unclear.
connections.assumptions: Informal interactions create professional networks vs. purely social connections without career impact. Evidence basis: Testimonials mention collaborative projects and ongoing professional relationships post-residency. Testability: Survey alumni on professional collaborations, job referrals, and ongoing project partnerships originating from residency connections 12-24 months post-departure. Risk factors: Community dynamics may exclude introverted individuals, groupthink in small closed community, geographic isolation limits external network integration. Historical precedent: Coworking spaces and intentional communities show positive networking effects but with high individual variation.

nodes.title: Individuals develop foundational knowledge and skills in EA cause areas
nodes.text: Residents build domain expertise, research capabilities, and practical skills relevant to priority cause work

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Examples of residents publishing research and joining EA organizations post-residency; (2) Case studies of skill development in AI safety, global health research; (3) Career transition testimonials. Quality note: Limited systematic tracking of skill development measurement or career outcome attribution to residency vs. other factors.
connections.assumptions: Skill development during residency translates to long-term EA career commitment vs. temporary engagement. Evidence basis: Some alumni maintain EA careers, but systematic tracking limited. Testability: Longitudinal survey of alumni EA career engagement, skill utilization, and attribution factors 12, 24, 36 months post-residency. Risk factors: Skills developed may not match job market needs, motivation fades post-residency without ongoing support, alternative career opportunities provide better compensation/security. Historical precedent: Professional development programs show highly variable long-term career impact - success depends on ongoing support systems and market demand.

nodes.title: Early-career professionals form productive networks within EA community
nodes.text: Residents develop lasting professional relationships, mentorship connections, and collaborative partnerships

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Examples of residents co-founding organizations (case studies mention collaborative launches); (2) Network formation metrics suggest relationship building; (3) Alumni maintain connections post-residency based on testimonials. Quality note: Organizational founding rates not systematically tracked, co-founder relationships difficult to attribute specifically to residency networking.
connections.assumptions: Professional networks formed during residency enable organizational founding vs. networks being primarily social or only facilitating employment in existing organizations. Evidence basis: Some examples of collaborative organizational launches, but causation unclear. Testability: Track co-founding rates among alumni vs. control groups, survey network utilization for organizational development. Risk factors: Networks may be geographically concentrated in UK, small community size limits diversity of connections, network effects may primarily benefit extroverted personalities. Historical precedent: Elite educational/professional programs show strong network effects for organizational founding, but outcomes highly dependent on network diversity and ongoing maintenance.

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Network connections facilitate job opportunities in EA organizations (testimonials reference recommendations and referrals); (2) Mentorship relationships continue post-residency; (3) Average 12 meaningful connections suggests network density. Quality note: Job placement attribution to networking vs. individual qualifications difficult to separate.
connections.assumptions: EA community networks provide career opportunities vs. networks being primarily social support without professional advancement impact. Evidence basis: Testimonials reference job referrals and recommendations, EA job market appears network-dependent. Testability: Compare job placement rates and career advancement between networked vs. non-networked early-career EAs. Risk factors: Network effects may create insider advantages vs. merit-based selection, geographic concentration limits global opportunities, network value depends on network members' career success. Historical precedent: Professional networks show strong career impact in tight-knit communities like EA, but can create equity issues.

nodes.title: Individuals gain practical experience through focused project work
nodes.text: Residents complete research projects, organizational development, and cause-specific work with dedicated time and support

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Examples of research outputs produced during residencies (publications, reports); (2) Case studies of residents contributing to AI safety, global health research; (3) Website references research among resident activities. Quality note: Research quality and impact not systematically evaluated, publication rates compared to baseline unclear.
connections.assumptions: Project work during residency produces research contributions vs. primarily serving as learning exercises without external impact. Evidence basis: Some documented research outputs, but quality and impact assessment limited. Testability: Compare research output quality, citation rates, and impact assessments for residency-supported vs. independently conducted early-career research. Risk factors: Lack of institutional oversight may reduce research quality, isolation from academic/professional feedback reduces rigor, time pressure may incentivize quantity over quality. Historical precedent: Independent research fellowships show variable quality outcomes - success highly dependent on mentorship and institutional support structures.

nodes.title: Effective organizations addressing global priorities are founded and reach sustainable scale
nodes.text: Alumni launch new organizations working on AI safety, global health, animal welfare, and other EA priority areas

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Alumni organizational launches: AI Standards Lab, Convergence Analysis, ML4G, Electric Sheep, Orxl; (2) Organizations address EA priority causes (AI safety, research, global priorities); (3) Some organizations receiving funding/recognition within EA ecosystem. Quality note: Organizational sustainability and impact not systematically tracked long-term, founding attribution to CEEALAR unclear.
connections.assumptions: Organizations founded by alumni create additional impact vs. displacing activity that would have occurred anyway. Evidence basis: Organizations work on priorities areas, but counterfactual impact difficult to assess. Testability: Compare organizational founding rates and sustainability between CEEALAR alumni vs. matched control groups of early-career EAs. Risk factors: High organizational failure rates generally, EA funding concentration may not support multiple similar organizations, founder inexperience reduces sustainability likelihood. Historical precedent: Startup accelerators show most new organizations fail within 5 years - success depends heavily on founder experience, market conditions, and ongoing support.

nodes.title: Rigorous research produces actionable evidence on high-impact interventions
nodes.text: Alumni contribute research that identifies, validates, and improves understanding of effective approaches to global priorities

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Some research outputs by alumni during/after residency; (2) Research focuses on EA priority areas (AI safety, global health, cause prioritization); (3) Integration with broader EA research community. Quality note: Research impact assessment not systematic, citation analysis not available, contribution to field advancement unclear.
connections.assumptions: Individual research contributions aggregate to influence field understanding vs. research being primarily developmental without field-level impact. Evidence basis: Limited evidence of research uptake or citation by broader research community. Testability: Track research publication rates, citation impacts, and uptake by other researchers/organizations for alumni vs. control groups. Risk factors: Early-career researchers typically have limited impact, EA research community may be too small for specialization, research may duplicate existing work without novel contributions. Historical precedent: Early-career research typically has minimal short-term impact - field influence usually requires extended experience and institutional affiliation.

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Research on cause prioritization and intervention effectiveness produced by some alumni; (2) Integration with EA decision-making processes through forum posts, organizational reports. Quality note: Limited evidence of research directly influencing funding allocation decisions.
connections.assumptions: Research findings influence resource allocation decisions vs. research being primarily academic without policy/funding impact. Evidence basis: EA community emphasizes evidence-based decision making, but direct influence of specific research on funding unclear. Testability: Track citations of alumni research in funding decisions, policy changes, or organizational strategy documents. Risk factors: Research may be too theoretical for practical application, decision makers may rely on more established sources, timing misalignment between research completion and decision cycles. Historical precedent: Policy research shows limited direct influence on decisions - impact typically requires sustained engagement and institutional relationships.

nodes.title: Qualified individuals develop expertise and commit careers to priority cause areas
nodes.text: Alumni build substantial expertise and maintain long-term engagement in EA cause areas through career choices

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Examples of alumni joining EA organizations in research and operational roles; (2) Career transition testimonials showing sustained EA engagement; (3) Professional development evident in LinkedIn profiles and organizational affiliations. Quality note: Systematic career tracking not available, attribution to CEEALAR vs. other factors unclear.
connections.assumptions: Career commitment translates to increased organizational capacity vs. individuals simply filling roles that would be filled by others anyway. Evidence basis: EA talent constraints suggest additional qualified individuals increase capacity, but substitution effects possible. Testability: Compare EA organization growth rates and capacity in areas with more vs. fewer CEEALAR alumni, survey organizations on hiring constraints and capacity impacts. Risk factors: High EA career burnout rates may reduce long-term commitment, compensation differentials may drive career changes, geographic concentration may limit global talent distribution. Historical precedent: Career development programs show variable long-term retention - success depends on ongoing professional development and community support.

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Alumni in EA organizations contribute to funding allocation decisions through research and advisory roles; (2) Career placements in grantmaking organizations and research roles; (3) Professional development suggests qualified participation in resource allocation. Quality note: Direct influence on funding decisions difficult to trace.
connections.assumptions: Individual career development aggregates to improve resource allocation quality vs. marginal individual contributions having negligible system-level impact. Evidence basis: EA funding increasingly emphasizes expertise and analysis, suggesting qualified professionals influence decisions. Testability: Compare funding allocation quality metrics (impact assessments, outcome tracking) in areas with more vs. fewer qualified EA professionals. Risk factors: Individual influence may be limited by institutional constraints, cognitive biases may persist despite expertise, resource allocation may be more influenced by political/social factors than technical expertise. Historical precedent: Professional development in policy/philanthropic sectors shows mixed evidence of improving decision quality - expertise necessary but not sufficient for better outcomes.

nodes.title: High-impact interventions addressing global priorities are implemented at scale
nodes.text: Effective programs addressing AI safety, global health, animal welfare, and existential risks reach substantial scale

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Historical examples of scaled interventions improving wellbeing (malaria bed nets, deworming programs, vaccine distribution); (2) EA research on intervention effectiveness and cost-effectiveness; (3) Organizational track records of scaling successful programs. Quality note: Strong evidence base from development economics and public health for intervention scaling effects.
connections.assumptions: Scaled interventions maintain effectiveness vs. diminishing returns or implementation challenges reducing impact. Evidence basis: Development literature shows many interventions maintain effectiveness at scale with proper implementation. Testability: Monitor intervention impact assessments, cost-effectiveness analyses, and outcome measurements as programs scale. Risk factors: Implementation challenges at scale, cultural adaptation requirements, funding sustainability, political/regulatory barriers. Historical precedent: Many global health interventions successfully scaled with maintained effectiveness (vaccines, water/sanitation, nutrition programs).

nodes.title: Existential risk probabilities are measurably reduced through effective mitigation
nodes.text: AI safety progress, biosecurity improvements, and other risk mitigation efforts demonstrably reduce probability of catastrophic outcomes

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Expert assessments linking existential risk reduction to prevention of catastrophic suffering; (2) Expected value calculations showing enormous impact of risk reduction; (3) Historical examples of successful risk mitigation (nuclear security, ozone depletion). Quality note: High confidence in connection between risk reduction and wellbeing, though risk quantification remains uncertain.
connections.assumptions: Risk mitigation efforts successfully reduce actual risk vs. merely improving risk perception or measurement. Evidence basis: Some historical precedents of successful risk mitigation, though existential risks have limited historical precedent. Testability: Monitor expert risk assessments, technical milestone achievements, institutional capacity indicators. Risk factors: Risk displacement rather than reduction, measurement challenges for novel risks, coordination failures despite technical progress. Historical precedent: Mixed success in risk mitigation - some successes (nuclear security, environmental protection) but also failures (climate change, pandemic preparedness).

nodes.title: Resources flow to the most cost-effective interventions
nodes.text: Funding, talent, and attention are allocated based on evidence of impact rather than traditional or emotional factors

connections.evidence: Multi-source evidence: (1) Economic research on efficiency gains from better resource allocation; (2) EA research documenting large differences in intervention cost-effectiveness; (3) Examples of improved outcomes from evidence-based funding allocation. Quality note: Strong theoretical foundation, some empirical evidence from EA and development sectors.
connections.assumptions: Evidence-based allocation actually improves outcomes vs. existing allocation mechanisms already incorporating relevant information through other channels. Evidence basis: EA research shows large cost-effectiveness differences suggesting allocation improvements possible. Testability: Compare outcome improvements in funding areas with more vs. less evidence-based allocation processes. Risk factors: Evidence may be incomplete or biased, local knowledge may outweigh general evidence, implementation challenges may negate theoretical advantages. Historical precedent: Evidence-based medicine and development aid show improvements from better allocation, but implementation remains challenging.

nodes.title: Improved wellbeing for all sentient beings (present and future)
nodes.text: Reduced suffering and increased flourishing across humans, animals, and future generations through effective interventions and risk mitigation
